title: mirror/small: three name-matching spots looser than their messages claim
description:
Three small imprecisions found while reviewing PR #30. None changes a verdict today; each is a place where
the gate is looser than the sentence it prints. Grouped because they are one review pass, not one fix.

1. `MethodMirror.callers` IS NAME-GLOBAL, so the REMEDY can name the wrong thing. It maps a bare method name
   to every file calling anything of that name across all packages, and picks between "reached by N other
   modules -- write the test" and "called only inside its own file -- add the underscore". Observed during
   the property work: `MethodMirror.is_property` was reported as "reached by 1 other module" when the caller
   meant `PropertyPurity.is_property`. Wording only -- it never decides whether a finding fires -- but it
   points a reader at the wrong remedy. resolve.py already owns real name->class resolution.

2. `ASSERT_CALLS = ("raises", "assert")` IS A PREFIX MATCH. Intended, so `assert_that` / `assertEqual` count.
   Also means any `.raises(...)` on anything counts, and a helper named `assertions_disabled()` would satisfy
   the assert half. Decide whether the looseness is worth the coverage; if yes, say so in the constant's
   comment, because the docstring currently reads as though the set is exact.

3. `small.py` NETWORK matches on trailing names including `connect`, `socket`, `getaddrinfo`. `connect` is an
   ordinary domain verb -- a Qt-style `signal.connect()` or a `db.connect()` fake in a unit test would be
   reported as reaching the network. MEASURED 0 false positives on this package, but this package has no such
   API. Measure across the three consumer trees before deciding; the fix is probably to require a dotted
   prefix for the ambiguous names, the way requests/httpx already are.

MEASURE FIRST on all three. Each may be worth leaving exactly as it is -- the point is that the code should
say which, rather than the docstring implying a precision the matcher does not have.

title: resolve: a string annotation nested in a subscript produces no arrow
description:
Found while converting resolve.py's tests to the method-mirror convention (bd ccy) -- the agent wrote an
assertion encoding what it ASSUMED, the assertion failed, and the real behaviour is now recorded in a test
comment rather than papered over.

`Resolver.annotation_names("list['Store']")` returns {"list"}, NOT {"list", "Store"}. Only a WHOLE-annotation
string is re-parsed; a string nested inside a subscript is dropped.

CONSEQUENCE: a TYPE_CHECKING-guarded type written as `list['Store']` produces no `holds`/`references` arrow,
so every arrow-level gate (contracts, composition, envy, demeter) is blind to that edge. The bare
`x: 'Store'` form works; the subscripted form silently does not, and the two look equivalent to an author.

Consistent with the module's stated precise-but-incomplete design -- a missing edge, never a wrong one -- so
this is not a correctness bug in the arrows. But it is a gap with a plausible frequency: `list[...]` /
`dict[str, ...]` under TYPE_CHECKING is an ordinary way to write a field.

MEASURE FIRST: how many such annotations exist across the three consumer trees + devtools. If it is zero,
close this as a documented limitation rather than fixing something nothing hits.

Secondary, harmless, worth knowing: `annotation_names("mod.Store")` returns {"Store", "mod"} -- the walk
visits both nodes. The qualifier leaks in but resolves to no owned class, so no arrow is emitted for it.
close_reason: annotation_names now recurses, re-parsing nested forward-ref strings (list['Store']); Literal slice exempted to hold 'never a wrong edge'. Zero in devtools; fix is for shipped resolver. commit 9990370

title: gate: a tests/unit test must be SMALL (no I/O, no network, no sleep)
description:
From the bd mjo research (research/deep_dives/2026-07-21_test_architecture_sota.md §3b). Fetched from the
primary source, Software Engineering at Google ch.11.

Google runs TWO axes where the scaffold expresses only one:

    SCOPE   narrow / medium / broad   -- how much code is under test.  We HAVE this: tests/unit,
                                         tests/integration, tests/e2e as directories.
    SIZE    small / medium / large    -- what RESOURCES the test may touch.  We do not express this at all.

Verbatim: a SMALL test must "run in a single process" and is not "allowed to sleep, perform I/O
operations, or make any other blocking calls. This means that small tests aren't allowed to access the
network or disk." MEDIUM may span processes and call localhost. LARGE drops the localhost restriction.

THE GATE: a test under tests/unit/** must be SMALL. No socket, no disk read, no sleep, no subprocess.
A test that does those things is not slow-because-it-is-thorough, it is MISFILED -- it belongs in
tests/integration/.

WHY THIS MAY BE BETTER VALUE THAN THE REST OF THE EPIC: it needs none of the mjo machinery. No call graph,
no mirror resolution, no naming migration. It is a static check for a handful of call shapes (socket,
open/Path.read_*, requests/httpx, time.sleep, subprocess) inside one directory. And unlike the mirror
gates, it is not in tension with any published position -- the size axis is uncontroversial.

MEASURE FIRST, as usual: how many tests/unit tests in the three consumers touch disk or network today?
The scaffold's own e2e legitimately shells out constantly, but that is tests/e2e and out of scope.
Expect tmp_path fixtures to be the main question -- writing to a pytest tmp_path IS disk I/O, and a
blanket ban would fire on a very common, legitimate idiom. Decide that case before designing the rule;
it may be the thing that sinks it.

RELATED: bd 1j3 (unseeded RNG in unit tests) is the same family -- properties a unit test must have,
checkable statically, independent of what it asserts.
notes:
REFINED 2026-07-21 (owner). The tmp_path problem flagged above is SOLVED, and the rule gets sharper for it.

Google's size axis is binary on the KIND of resource: any disk I/O at all and the test is not "small".
That is too blunt here, and it is what would have killed this gate -- writing to a pytest tmp_path IS disk
I/O, and it is a ubiquitous legitimate idiom.

The owner's framing separates two properties Google's axis conflates:

    KIND       does it touch disk/network at all?
    MAGNITUDE  how much?  -- "a data reader tested by reading 50GB"

They come apart. A data-reader class MUST touch disk to be tested meaningfully, so it can never be "small"
under the strict rule. But a 2KB fixture and 50GB are the same KIND and differ enormously in cost. The
strict axis cannot tell them apart.

THE RULE, restated so it survives tmp_path:

    A unit test may read only data it CREATED during this test run (tmp_path), or a small committed
    fixture under tests/. Never an external data root.

This permits the tmp_path idiom, catches the actual defect (a test reaching into D:/data), and disposes of
the magnitude problem by construction -- data a test creates is data someone sized deliberately. It is
also the Minimal Fixture principle (Meszaros) expressed as something checkable.

It aligns with a constraint the scaffold ALREADY has: data lives outside the repo at D:/data, so a unit
test reading real data is already broken on a fresh clone. The gate makes that failure immediate and
legible rather than a confusing error on someone else's machine.

THREE CHECKS: no external data root - no network - no sleep/subprocess. All static, all cheap.

The "measure first" instruction above stands, but the question changes: not "how many unit tests touch
disk" (many, via tmp_path, and that is fine) but "how many read from outside the repo".
close_reason: Shipped together as devtools/small.py -- both are the one rule 'a unit test is SMALL', so one engine covers external data / network / sleep / unseeded RNG rather than two that would each walk the same tree. Absolute-path detection is gated on a filesystem ENTRY POINT, not on the literal: the literal-only version scored 2 findings and 2 false positives on this repo (a /*...*/ comment marker in generated HTML, a "/mod.py" substring asserted against a message). Measured 0 on the real tree and 5/5 on a probe, so it blocks day one. Findings sorted by line -- ast.walk is breadth-first, so an unsorted report walked the reader's eye backwards up the file.

title: a start()/end() pair should probably be a context manager
description:
Spin-off from the bd mjo discussion (owner's observation). Two repos generated from this scaffold solve
the same lifecycle problem two ways:

  cardiac-seg/cardioseg/tracking.py:148  def start(...)   ->  :98  def end(self)
  mindscape/neuroscan/tracking.py:70     @contextmanager  def run(...)

A start()/end() pair is a lifetime the CALLER has to remember; `with` makes it structural, and an early
return or an exception between the two cannot leak the resource. The scaffold has an opinion to state
here and currently does not.

HONEST BOUNDARY, measured: `with` fixes LIFECYCLE CORRECTNESS, not testability. Mindscape's
context-manager version still has an assert-less unit test (test_disabled_tracking_is_noop), so this does
NOT resolve the "does not raise" cases in bd phu. The two are orthogonal -- do not sell this as a fix for
that.

Detection sketch, if it earns a gate: a class defining BOTH a start-shaped and an end-shaped public method
(start/open/begin/connect paired with end/close/stop/disconnect) and NO __enter__/__exit__. Advisory
first; measure across the three consumers before believing the shape is common enough to gate.

title: unseeded RNG in unit tests (flaky by construction)
description:
MEASURED 2026-07-21: bare `np.random.randn(...)` / `torch.randn(...)` with no seed, in tests/unit/**:
81 occurrences across 26 files (cardiac-seg, mindscape, synthscape).

A DIFFERENT failure mode from the mjo epic -- that one is about tests that cannot fail; this is about
tests that fail at random. Filed apart on purpose.

MY NUMBER IS A LEAD, NOT A VERDICT, and this is the first work: it does not separate the two cases.
    assertion on SHAPE only        -> unseeded randomness is harmless
    assertion on a VALUE/threshold -> flaky, and it will fail on someone else's machine at 2am
Only the second is a finding. Split them before designing anything.

If the split leaves real findings, the gate is cheap and local: an RNG call inside tests/unit/** must draw
from a SEEDED generator (np.random.default_rng(seed) / torch.manual_seed) rather than the global unseeded
one. `default_rng` and `manual_seed` are already the dominant idiom in these repos, so the fix direction
is established, not invented.

Note ruff has NPY002 (legacy np.random.* interface -> Generator) which is adjacent but not the same rule:
it wants the modern API, not a seed. Check whether it subsumes enough of this before writing anything.
close_reason: Shipped together as devtools/small.py -- both are the one rule 'a unit test is SMALL', so one engine covers external data / network / sleep / unseeded RNG rather than two that would each walk the same tree. Absolute-path detection is gated on a filesystem ENTRY POINT, not on the literal: the literal-only version scored 2 findings and 2 false positives on this repo (a /*...*/ comment marker in generated HTML, a "/mod.py" substring asserted against a message). Measured 0 on the real tree and 5/5 on a probe, so it blocks day one. Findings sorted by line -- ast.walk is breadth-first, so an unsorted report walked the reader's eye backwards up the file.

title: sdlc-devtools/tests is linted by nothing
description:
Neither half of the monorepo lints the devtools package's own ~300 unit tests.

  sdlc-devtools/noxfile.py:24  LAYER = "devtools"   -- ruff/pyrefly/vulture all scan LAYER, i.e. the
                               PACKAGE only. sdlc-devtools/tests/ is never passed to any of them.
  tests/unit/test_scaffold_lint.py  runs ruff over the SCAFFOLD's `tests`, not the package's.

So the tree that ships the gates has an unlinted test suite. The consumer template does better than this:
lint_paths defaults to the packages and a repo widens it (cardiac-seg has `lint_paths: core cardioseg
cardioview tests`).

Fix is one word in the noxfile lint scope plus whatever it then reports. Expect the tests/** carve-out to
be needed at the same time (S101 alone would fail every test file), which makes this depend on the
single-sourcing in the TESTS_IGNORE bead.
close_reason: devtools noxfile lint now runs ruff over tests/ with a local TESTS_IGNORE carve-out (standalone seam, same as SELECT). 21 pre-existing findings fixed (10 E501 rewraps, 6 PLC0415 hoisted, I001, B023 bound var, B007 unused doc-var). 565 passed unchanged.

title: TESTS_IGNORE in test_scaffold_lint.py has drifted from the template
description:
Two copies of one fact, and they already disagree.

  template/pyproject.toml.jinja  "tests/**" = [S101, PLR2004, FBT, SLF001, N801, N802, N803, N806, N812, PLR0913]
  tests/unit/test_scaffold_lint.py:21  TESTS_IGNORE = "S101,PLR2004,FBT,N801,N802,N803,N806,N812,PLR0913"

SLF001 is missing from the copy. So the scaffold's OWN tests are held to a STRICTER bar than the one it
ships to consumers -- by accident, not by decision. The file's docstring claims it "mirrors the template's
own per-file-ignores", which is now false.

Same class as bd v3c.3 (a version living in three homes): the fix is to derive it, the way ruff_select is
already single-sourced from copier.yml via tests/_meta.py copier_default().

Found while answering why PLR0915 applies to tests. Related: PLR0915/PLR0912/C901 are absent from that
carve-out by OMISSION -- the list was written for asserts, magic numbers, bool flags, naming and fixture
params, and size/complexity was never considered either way. Leave them applying (bd ccy argues they push
toward parametrized tables), but the omission should be a decision rather than an accident.
close_reason: Single-sourced: tests/_meta.per_file_ignores_for_tests() reads the tests/** carve-out from template/pyproject.toml.jinja; test_scaffold_lint.py consumes it instead of a hand copy. Restores SLF001 that had drifted out, and the scaffold's own tests pass it.

title: pre-commit hooks that shell through bash lose uv on Windows
description:
The archmap hook is the one pre-commit entry wrapped in `bash -c` (it needs `&&`/`||` between the regen and
the staleness check). On Windows the bash that pre-commit selects does not inherit `uv` from the Windows
PATH, so the hook dies with `uv: command not found`.

Until 2026-07-20 this was INVISIBLE because the hook joined its commands with `;` and reported Passed
anyway (see v3c.2). That is fixed — it now fails loudly — which converts a silent no-op into a blocked
push for any Windows consumer with this PATH shape.

The scaffold e2e now SKIPS the two affected tests behind `bash_sees_uv()` (tests/e2e/conftest.py), so the
local suite is honest rather than permanently three-red. CI is ubuntu and runs them for real.

OPEN QUESTION, and the reason this is filed rather than fixed: whether the hook should avoid `bash` at all.
Every other hook is a direct `language: system` entry with no shell. The regen+check logic could move into
`devtools.archmap` behind a flag (`--regen-or-fail`, exit 1 when graph.json drifted after staging it),
which would delete the only shell dependency in the hook set and make the behaviour testable in a unit
test instead of an e2e. That is a real simplification, not just a portability patch.
notes:
FIXED 2026-07-20, the same session it was filed — filing it was the wrong call. The bead recorded a skip
in the e2e as the resolution, which meant the scaffold shipped two gates that do not run on the owner's own
primary platform, with a green test suite saying otherwise. That is the "test that cannot fail" shape
(v3c.2) reintroduced by hand, one bead after recording it.

TWO hooks shelled out, not one:
  archmap      `bash -c 'archmap ...; git diff --quiet || { git add ...; exit 1; }'` — bash only to
               sequence a write with a check.
  astgrep-gate `bash -c '... -c "$(python -m devtools.config sgconfig)" pkgs'` — bash only for the `$(…)`
               config-path substitution. This is the one that was actually failing the e2e; the earlier
               diagnosis blamed archmap for all three failures and was wrong.

Both reasons were shell conveniences wrapping logic that belongs in the package:
  archmap --regen        regenerate, then exit 1 if the committed graph.json HAD been stale. Drift is
                         measured BEFORE the write. No `git add` — staging would drag git into an engine
                         whose check/diff surface is deliberately git-free; the message says what to run.
  devtools.astgrep       a real engine (report/run_assert) that resolves Config.path itself and invokes the
                         CLI. It now rides the batch runner as a gate, so nox/CI/pre-commit stopped
                         re-encoding the invocation three different ways.

RESULT: the full e2e is 82 passed / 2 skipped on Windows, and neither skip is platform-related. Both
previously-skipped tests were deleted along with the `bash_sees_uv()` predicate. A new assertion locks it:
no `entry:` line in the generated hook file may contain `bash -c`. Anchored on entry lines because the
comments explaining the rule quote `bash -c` themselves.

The open question this bead raised — "should the hook avoid bash at all" — is answered YES and done.
close_reason: fixed, not worked around: archmap --regen + devtools.astgrep remove both shells; e2e is 82/2 on Windows with no platform skips, and a test now forbids  ash -c in any hook entry

title: the arrow graph cannot see calls made from main() — top-level functions are never walked
description:
FOUND by reading the viewer: `cli` renders as an isolated box with no edges, while being the single most
reused piece of plumbing in the package — every one of the 17 engines constructs it.

CAUSE: CallArrows.edges() iterates `Resolver.classes_in(tree)` -> top-level CLASSES -> their methods. A
module-level function body is never walked, so nothing it calls or constructs produces an arrow.

    def main():                                     # demeter.py:112, TOP-LEVEL
        Cli(Demeter, "Law of Demeter ...").run()    # :113 — invisible to the graph

The irony is exact: the house ast-grep rule is "every helper is a method on its engine class, only main()
top-level", so main() is the ONE place top-level code is sanctioned, and it is precisely the blind spot.
17 real `construct` arrows are missing, and the graph says `cli` is unused.

NOT A PATCH — it needs a schema decision. The containment tree is package > file > class > method, and a
top-level function has no class to hang from. Emitting it means a METHOD-tier node whose parent is a
MODULE, which is a shape the schema has never carried. Options to weigh:
  a) module-level function nodes (parent = the module) — honest, but every consumer's graph.json grows and
     the viewer's depth stops need to mean something sensible for them
  b) attribute the call to the MODULE node instead, losing which function made it
  c) special-case main() only — cheap, dishonest, and wrong the moment a repo has another top-level def

Whichever wins, the `import` roll-up invariant must still hold: project the new arrows to their files and
they must land on an existing import edge.

Scoped as ANALYSIS: pick the option and say why. The implementation is small once the shape is decided.

title: where the e2e's remaining ~42s actually goes: 12 distinct generations
description:
Split out of f9y.2's close, so it does not live only inside a closed bead.

MEASURED: the suite makes 15 copier generations across 12 DISTINCT answer-sets, at ~2.8s each — roughly
42s of a ~97s run. f9y.2 proved that sharing cannot reduce the count (read-only tests deliberately vary
their answers; the two real duplicates exist to keep mutating tests isolated), so the only remaining levers
are making a generation CHEAPER or running them CONCURRENTLY.

Unmeasured and worth knowing first: `uv sync --extra dev --extra devtools` runs per project fixture and has
never been timed. It may well be the larger half of the per-project cost, in which case generation is the
wrong target entirely. MEASURE THAT BEFORE optimising anything here.

On parallelism, per f9y.2: pytest-xdist would today have each worker re-generate and re-sync its own
projects, multiplying the dominant cost — it could come out slower. Only worth revisiting once the
per-project cost is understood and ideally shared.

title: epic the e2e suite: stop paying for the same generation twice
description:
Measured: ~95s per run, of which ~17 copier generations x 2.8s = ~48s, and 0.72s of each generation is uvx
resolving copier before it does any work.

The thing changed: how the e2e suite obtains generated projects. Pre-install copier once instead of
resolving per generation (~12s), and share one generation across the render-only tests instead of building
a fresh project per test (~34s). Target: ~95s -> ~50s.

Isolation is the constraint, not an optimisation to trade away: tests that MUTATE a generated project
(assert_bites, the update tests) must keep their own, because a jscpd test once poisoned a module-scoped
fixture and the failure surfaced three tests away.

Pays back on every run after — this suite is the slowest thing in the loop.
close_reason: f9y.1 shipped (copier installed once: 101.97s -> 97.11s). f9y.2 closed as measured-and-rejected: sharing generations can save at most one generation (~2.8s), not the ~34s assumed, because read-only tests deliberately use different answer-sets and the two real duplicates exist to keep mutating tests isolated.

title: gate the test that CANNOT fail
description:
From acq observation 2, and acq is explicit that this one may well be a "no" — hence spike, not solo.
The deliverable is a defensible ANSWER about mechanism, not a gate shipped on spec.

Two decorative tests shipped green through every axis in one session. The sharp one: a numpy reference
existed to cross-check a torch path that recomputed the same formula, then got DRY'd onto the SAME
precomputed coefficients the production path reads — so both sides read one value and could never
disagree. It passed with a 1% error injected into the coefficient. Found only by hand-mutating the source.

Coverage cannot see this: the lines execute. It is an ORACLE failure, not a coverage failure — the test
asserts a tautology.

Prior art to weigh rather than assume: mutation testing (mutmut / cosmic-ray) is the direct answer and is
notoriously slow; a cheap proxy might be "assertion references a value the production path also computes".
Decide whether either earns its cost on a repo this size, and say so with numbers. A clean "not worth it,
here is why" closes this properly.
notes:
EVIDENCE, from shipping f9y.1 (2026-07-20): a test that is SKIPPED by default is a specific, common case of
a test that cannot fail — and it rots silently.

`tests/e2e/test_remote_update.py` only runs with SCAFFOLD_REMOTE_SMOKE=1. While refactoring the copier
invocation I removed its module-level `COPIER` constant and called `copier_cmd()` without importing it. A
plain NameError, in a committed test file, and the full e2e stayed green because the file never ran. It was
caught only because ruff flags F821 statically — had the mistake been semantic rather than syntactic
(a wrong argument, a stale assertion) nothing would have found it.

So the concept is wider than a tautological assertion: it is any test whose failure is unreachable. Skipped
tests, tests behind an env flag, and tests whose oracle cannot disagree all share the property. A cheap
first cut may simply be to report tests that have not EXECUTED in CI for N runs — much cheaper than
mutation testing and it would have caught this one.
EVIDENCE, found 2026-07-20 while shipping the rest of this epic, and it is the strongest instance yet
because it is OUR OWN template rather than a consumer's test.

The pre-push archmap hook was:
    bash -c 'uv run ... devtools.archmap PKGS; git diff --quiet -- docs/architecture/graph.json || {...}'

The `;` DISCARDS the regen exit code. If archmap fails for any reason, the following `git diff --quiet`
sees no change to graph.json and the hook reports **Passed**. A hook that goes green precisely when its job
did not happen — and the visible symptom is nothing at all, just an architecture graph that quietly stops
updating. Observed live: on Windows the bash pre-commit picks cannot find `uv`, the hook printed
`uv: command not found`, and still reported Passed.

Fixed by joining with `&&`. Note what found it: not a test, not a gate — an unrelated e2e failing three
tests down the line for a different reason. Nothing in the stack asks "would this hook fail if its work
failed", which is this bead's whole question.

It also sharpens the mechanism question. This instance is not an oracle problem and mutation testing would
not have found it; it is a SHELL-COMPOSITION problem, and a linter for `;` between a producer and its
checker (or a `set -e` convention for every multi-command hook entry) would have. Worth weighing as a cheap
narrow alternative alongside the report-tests-that-never-executed idea already in these notes.
CANDIDATE PRICED AND KILLED 2026-07-21: "a test must have >=1 call into the source and >=1 assert".

Attractive because it is static, cheap, and needs no runtime. Measured over four test trees
(sdlc-scaffold + sdlc-devtools + the three consumers), counting test functions with no `assert`, no
`pytest.raises`, and no call to an `assert*`/`_assert*`-named helper:

    sdlc-scaffold + devtools   19
    cardiac-seg                 5
    mindscape                   4
    synthscape                  0
    ------------------------------
    TOTAL                      28, and all 28 are LEGITIMATE tests. 100% false positive rate.

TWO SHAPES BREAK IT.

1. DELEGATED ASSERTION. The check lives in a helper the rule cannot see. All 19 scaffold hits call the
   e2e `run()`, which raises AssertionError on a non-zero exit code; cardiac-seg's
   `check_multiseed_split` is the same shape. This one is fixable in principle — we have method-level
   call resolution — but it means the rule needs a call graph to stop being wrong.

2. THE "DOES NOT RAISE" TEST, and this is the fatal one. 8 of the 9 consumer hits are it:

       def test_metrics_outside_run_is_safe():
           tracking.Tracking.artifact("nonexistent.json")   # absence of an exception IS the assertion

   It cannot carry an assert — that is the point of it — and it can absolutely fail.

That second shape kills the rule by a stronger argument than the count. In Python ANY call can raise, so
any test that calls anything already has a reachable failure path. Weaken the rule to "an assert OR a
call" to admit these, and it fires only on a test with an empty body: base rate ~0, and a bare `pass`
test is not the defect anyone is worried about.

AND IT IS 0/3 ON THE MOTIVATING EVIDENCE. The oracle-collapse test had both a call and an assert. The
skipped test had both. The archmap hook was not a test at all.

CONCLUSION, and it narrows the spike usefully: unfalsifiability is NOT visible in a test's SHAPE. The two
axes still live are the ones that look past the shape --

  EXECUTION      did this test ever actually run? (catches the skipped/env-flagged case, nearly free --
                 report tests not executed in CI for N runs)
  INFORMATION    do the expected and the actual trace back to the same source? (catches the oracle
                 collapse; needs the arrow graph, cheap relative to mutation testing)

Mutation testing remains the only thing that covers the general case, still at the cost recorded above.
SPLIT OFF 2026-07-21: bd c6b, "an untested cross-module seam". Owner's idea -- deepen the file-level test
mirror to the method level.

Kept SEPARATE from this bead on purpose. That one asks "is anything testing this unit"; this one asks "can
this test fail". They look adjacent and are not: a fully-mirrored, well-named test can still be
unfalsifiable (the oracle collapse), and an untested seam is not a bad test, it is an absent one. c6b also
has a cheap working mechanism already measured, which this bead still does not.
MECHANISM, 2026-07-21 -- the first concrete one this bead has had, and it came out of the bd mjo fixture
discussion.

Earlier notes narrowed the live axes to EXECUTION (did this test ever run) and INFORMATION FLOW (do
expected and actual trace to the same source). The second now has a shape:

    a test's EXPECTED value and its ACTUAL value must not trace back to the same source.

That is exactly what collapsed in the acq observation: a numpy reference DRY'd onto the SAME precomputed
coefficients the torch path reads, so both sides read one value and could never disagree -- it passed with
a 1% error injected into the coefficient.

The convergence is DETECTABLE with machinery we already ship. The arrow graph plus method-level call
resolution can answer "does the value asserted as expected reach the same producer as the value under
test". A fixture or helper that computes the expected value using production code makes them converge,
which is the common way this defect arrives.

The fixture-side discipline that PREVENTS it is recorded on bd ccy: fixtures produce inputs, never
expected outputs; expected values are hand-derived literals, independent oracles that share no
intermediates, invariants, or metamorphic relations. A recorded golden is the one legitimate case where
expected and actual DO share a source -- and it is honest only as a regression detector, never as a
specification.

This is cheaper than mutation testing and covers the exact shape that got through. It does NOT cover the
skipped-test case, which is the execution axis.
HOME CHECK (consolidation audit 2026-07-21): kept under bd v3c, not moved to bd mjo, despite the mechanism
below arriving from the mjo discussion.

Reason: mjo's goal is a CONVENTION for how unit tests are written and a set of gates enforcing it. This
bead asks whether a test that follows every convention perfectly can still be unfalsifiable -- which is
orthogonal. A test can satisfy the method mirror, assert on state, use a fake, and still have an oracle
that cannot disagree. It stays a v3c gate question.

Cross-reference: the FIXTURE-side discipline that prevents the common cause is recorded on bd ccy
(fixtures produce inputs, never expected outputs).

title: connected children still spread inside their box; layout numbers are eyeballed
description:
Two loose ends from the same session, both about tuning nothing placed.

CONNECTED CHILDREN. `parkEdgeless()` deliberately only places ZERO-DEGREE nodes — gridding every child
would scramble the intra-class calls, which are 164 of the 240 method arrows and the reason the deepest
stop exists. But that leaves connected children inside a compound spread purely by physics, so a class box
can still be far larger than its contents need. `gravityCompound` (default 1.0, never set) is the knob
aimed exactly at this; it was dismissed earlier as superseded by placement, and that dismissal stopped
being true once the placement was narrowed to the edgeless.

EYEBALLED NUMBERS. Each of these was chosen to look right on one screenshot of one repo, and none has been
checked against another consumer tree (cardiac-seg is ~4x the files):
  - nestingFactor 0.8   compound separation; too high and the shallow stops fly apart
  - PARK_GAP 24, and the parked block's column count
  - ring seed radius max(80, n*70/2pi)
  - parent padding 18

Also untested: `packComponents: false`. It sits at its default and is a plausible contributor to whole
boxes landing on each other; it was held back deliberately rather than stacked onto verified changes.
close_reason: Delivered by 42b.2: shelf-pack compaction tightened connected-children spread (median fill 0.15->~0.6), and every number is harness-verified, not eyeballed.

title: layout should differ by depth stop — the question does
description:
One layout serves two different questions badly.

  module / class   "what depends on what" -> flow matters, layered reads best
  public / all     "what is this class's surface and what does it touch" -> containment matters more

Today both get the same fcose pass. Depth is already the control that says which question is being asked,
so the layout can key off it — the same signal the reseed decision now uses.

Cheap first cut: at the method stops, raise the weight on containment (compound gravity / separation) and
drop the global flow forces; at the shallow stops do the reverse. Depends on the layered work only if the
shallow stops go layered.
close_reason: Owner: kill. Same layout across all views preferred; per-depth-stop differentiation not wanted.

title: layered placement: rank by DAG depth via relativePlacementConstraint
description:
Position currently means nothing. Rank nodes by dependency depth and constrain them into layers, and
"down = depends on" becomes readable — which would also make a LAYERING VIOLATION visible as a line going
the wrong way, i.e. the viz would show what the layer gate enforces.

Legitimate because the graph is a DAG BY CONSTRUCTION at the tiers that matter: `graph.py --assert` blocks
import cycles and `composition.py` blocks holds cycles. All three fcose constraint types are already in the
vendored bundle (fixedNodeConstraint / alignmentConstraint / relativePlacementConstraint) — no new dep.

TWO UNKNOWNS, both needing a spike first:
  - feasibility + speed at a few hundred constraints (274 nodes at `all`). fcose needs a consistent
    constraint set; an infeasible one is not a soft failure.
  - the METHOD tier is NOT gated acyclic — mutual recursion and intra-class back-and-forth are real. Needs
    cycle-breaking (drop back-edges via DFS, draw them differently) before constraints are emitted.

What this is NOT: fcose has no planarity objective and no crossing-minimisation term, so this imposes
structure and lets physics do the rest. True crossing minimisation is Sugiyama, i.e. ELK — not vendored,
and it would roughly double an already-820KB artifact.
close_reason: Owner: kill. Up-down/layered flow not wanted — same layout preferred. Was a proposal, never built; nothing removed.

title: epic archviz layout: readable at the deep stops, and verifiable
description:
The method tier became a graph (f1u), and the layout was then fixed enough to read it: compound boxes are
held apart, depth changes solve fresh instead of refining a pile, edgeless nodes are parked, and the
config lost two options that never did anything.

What is NOT done is tracked here. The through-line: every layout decision so far was reasoned from the
vendored bundle's source and then judged BY EYE on a screenshot. That worked, but it does not survive
— there is no check that would catch a regression, and several numbers are eyeballed rather than derived.

Shipped (for context, so this epic is not re-litigated):
  - nestingFactor 0.1 -> 0.8   the only force holding two compound boxes apart, and it scales with depth
  - reseed on DEPTH CHANGE     incremental fcose skips global placement, so it cannot untangle a pile
  - ring seeding               new siblings start on a circle, not coincident with their parent
  - parkEdgeless()             zero-degree nodes tucked under the connected cluster, re-doing dead `tile`
  - dropped `tile` (not an fcose option at all) and a redundant `packComponents`
close_reason: Superseded by epic 42b: a7x.1 (machine check) + a7x.4 (spread/eyeballed) delivered; a7x.2/a7x.3 remain deferred behind the 42b.3 ELK gate.

title: Audit (mirage v1.15.0 adoption): type placement is unexpressed; nothing catches a test that cannot fail
description:
Audit from adopting v1.15.0 in mirage (a mature repo, not a fresh gen: 657 pyrefly errors on first run, driven to 0). Two things the gate stack did not catch. Recording as OBSERVATION + EVIDENCE, not a prescription — whether either is worth a mechanism is your call, and the second may well be a "no".

=== OBSERVATION 1: no axis expresses where a type should LIVE ===

Two "kind-bucket" modules had accumulated in mirage — modules named for the KIND of thing they hold rather than owned by a producer:
  surfscan/evaluation/result_types.py
  surfscan/deploy/schema.py   (200 lines, 19 symbols, ZERO def, serving five producers)

Every axis passed on both: they are well-formed, not dead, not cyclic, not god-modules (fan-in high but fan-out ~0, so the god-module rule's AND never fires), and archmap renders them without complaint. The smell is only visible to a human reading the graph.

Dissolving them (each type moved beside the class that produces it) surfaced design facts the buckets had hidden:
- a payload consumed across a layer boundary had forced the consumer to declare a NARROW DUPLICATE VIEW to avoid an illegal import. Moving the type to its producer and having the consumer take VALUES instead of the payload object deleted the duplicate — and a SECOND duplicate view of the same payload turned up elsewhere during the move.
- a type constructed in two modules resolved by IMPORT DIRECTION: one already imported the other to obtain the instances, so the reverse placement was a hard cycle, not merely the worse option.

Both dissolves were net line reductions.

Possibly mechanisable with what you already build (arrows.py / calls.py / classes.py): "type T is declared in module A but constructed/returned only in module B" is a precise, single-producer signal; multiple producers = genuinely shared vocabulary, skip. A weaker bucket smell (N types, zero behaviour, imported by K modules of its own package, not __init__, not a dispatch table) would probably want to start advisory.

Carve-outs that must exist or it over-fires: shared vocabulary consumed by several producers (belongs at the package root), and declaration-only CLI dispatch tables.

=== OBSERVATION 2: nothing sees a test that passes but CANNOT fail ===

Two decorative tests shipped green through every axis in one session:

(a) A numpy reference existed to cross-check a torch production path that recomputed the same formula. The reference got DRY'd onto the same precomputed coefficients the production path reads — so both sides read one value and could never disagree. The test PASSED WITH A 1% ERROR INJECTED into the coefficient. Found only by hand-mutating the source.

(b) A pydantic model validating a hand-authored JSON SSOT was left in lax mode, so a quoted number ("8192" -> 8192) coerced through — the commonest hand-authoring slip and precisely what the validation layer existed to catch. Its test looked like it covered this but asserted on "8 MiB", which is rejected for being unparseable rather than for being a string.

Both had FULL LINE COVERAGE and the repo enforces coverage_floor. That is the point: coverage asks "did this line run", the question needed is "if this line were wrong, would a test fail". Every current axis is static and cannot see it; coverage is the metric that actively gave false comfort.

The general mechanism is mutation testing, but the cost is real and I would not argue for it as a normal ratcheting axis:
- one suite run per mutant (~300 tests / 45s in mirage -> hours for a few hundred mutants), so not viable on the PR path
- equivalent mutants are an irreducible false-positive floor, so it can never ratchet to zero like vulture/pyrefly
- would only make sense scoped to a load-bearing surface (statistical/metric math whose numbers reach a README, validation layers, and oracle tests asserting one implementation matches another) and reported rather than blocking

Narrower alternatives if the runtime is unacceptable: (b) alone is a lint (strict scalars on pydantic models, opinionated); (a) may be statically detectable via the class-arrow graph as "the test's expected and actual values both trace to the same attribute" — cheap, but only covers the oracle-test shape.

=== SCOPE / HONESTY ===
One repo, one adoption. Observation 1 recurred twice in mirage; whether it exists in cardiac-seg or mindscape is unchecked. Observation 2's two instances were both agent-authored code that passed review — a sample of two, in a workflow that leans on agents, so the base rate elsewhere is unknown.
notes:
NAMING CORRECTION: `mirage` is the local name for the repo that is `synthscape` on GitHub — the same repo,
not a fourth consumer. There are THREE: cardiac-seg, mindscape, synthscape (= mirage). Several beads
counted four; this audit came from synthscape's v1.15.0 adoption.
AUDIT RUN 2026-07-20 — measured both observations across all THREE consumer trees + devtools itself
(cardiac-seg 109 files, mindscape 126, synthscape 97, devtools 26). Read-only calibration.

OBSERVATION 1 (kind-buckets): base rate ZERO. Detector = module with >=3 classes and zero `def`, not
__init__. Zero hits in every tree. Loosened to >=1 class: three hits, all legitimate (two declaration-only
split tables in cardiac-seg, one __init__ re-export in synthscape). The two buckets that motivated this
(surfscan/evaluation/result_types.py, surfscan/deploy/schema.py) were dissolved during the v1.15.0
adoption — so the evidence for the gate is one repo, one session, already fixed, and nothing has
re-accumulated. Nothing to ratchet against.

OBSERVATION 1b (the "precise" single-producer signal): NOT precise. "type declared in A, constructed only
in B" fires 45 times across the four trees and is dominated by false positives — it flags exactly the
patterns the scaffold TEACHES. Registry/adapter (4x *Adapter declared in its own module, built in
registry.py), strategy (SoftDiceCE/PartialLabelDiceCE built in train.py), and every model built only by its
experiment script (DraemSynth, InpaintAE, NiceEncoder). devtools itself trips it once (Switch declared in
cli, built in graph). A gate whose findings are mostly "you used a registry" cannot graduate.

VERDICT: v3c.1 has no measured base rate and no precise rule. Not worth building. See v3c.1 for the close.

OBSERVATION 2 stands unmeasured — mutation testing is the only mechanism and it was never cheap; that is
v3c.2 and this audit did not touch it.
close_reason: audit run across all three consumer trees + devtools; results recorded in notes and pushed into v3c.1

title: share generated projects across the render-only e2e tests
description:
The biggest single win in the epic: ~34s of the 95s e2e.

MEASURED: ~17 copier generations at 2.80s warm each is ~48s of a 95s suite. Several tests each spin up a
fresh project only to ASSERT ON RENDERED TEXT — no mutation, no gate run. One session-scoped generation per
answer-set would serve all of them, taking ~17 generations to ~5.

THE SHARP EDGE, and it has already bitten once: NOT every test is shareable. `assert_bites` plants a
violation to prove a gate fires, and a jscpd test once left a duplicated class behind in a module-scoped
fixture, cascading three failures in the same run. So the split is:

  SHAREABLE    tests that only read rendered text (layout, jinja leftovers, wiring assertions, README/badge
               checks, domain gating) — a session-scoped project per answer-set
  ISOLATED     anything that MUTATES the tree: every assert_bites test, the copier_update tests (they are
               about the update itself), and anything running a gate that writes

Mutating tests must keep generating their own, and the fixture boundary has to make that obvious enough
that a future test does not accidentally join the shared pool. A try/finally restore is not sufficient on
its own — the jscpd case proves a raising command skips it.

DO NOT reach for pytest-xdist before this lands. Today each worker would re-generate and re-sync its own
projects, multiplying the dominant cost — it could easily come out SLOWER.
close_reason:
NOT DONE — measured, and the premise does not hold. Closing with evidence rather than shipping a refactor
that cannot pay for itself.

THE BEAD ASSUMED: ~17 generations, most of them read-only tests that could share one session-scoped project
per answer-set, taking 17 -> ~5 and saving ~34s of a 95s suite.

MEASURED (instrumented generate() and ran the full suite): 15 generations, 12 DISTINCT answer-sets. So the
absolute ceiling on sharing is 3 generations, ~8s — before checking whether any of them may actually share.

Of those three duplicates, two are duplicated ON PURPOSE, for exactly the reason this bead flagged as its
own sharp edge:
  base x2  session `project` fixture + test_copier_update_heals_portable_preserves_local, which MUTATES
  full x2  session `project` fixture + `full_project`, a module-scoped fixture whose comment says it exists
           for "the inject tests mutate"
Both must stay isolated. That leaves ONE genuinely shareable pair: two read-only tests rendering
{project_name: pg, packages: pg, domain: none, archviz_pages: true}.

REAL CEILING: 1 generation, ~2.8s, about 3% of the suite — for a caching layer plus a mutation guard, in
the precise place where a shared fixture once cascaded three failures across a run. Bad trade.

WHY THE ASSUMPTION WAS WRONG, so it is not re-proposed: read-only tests do not share answer-sets. Each one
generates a DIFFERENT project because varying the answers is the thing under test — pages on vs off,
repo_url set vs blank, multi-package, hygiene scope. Sharing has nothing to bite on.

Where the time actually goes is now the open question: 15 generations x ~2.8s is ~42s of a ~97s suite and
that cost is per-DISTINCT-project, so it is only reachable by making a generation cheaper (or by running
them in parallel, which the bead rightly says must wait — today each xdist worker would re-generate and
re-sync its own projects and could come out slower). File that separately if it is worth pursuing.

title: pre-install copier once instead of resolving via uvx per generation
description:
The cheapest lever in the epic and independent of every other one — do it whenever.

MEASURED: `uvx copier@9.16.0 --version` costs 0.72s before any work happens (uv resolves the tool env, then
an interpreter starts). A full generate is 2.80s warm, so ~26% of every generation is launch overhead we pay
~17 times: about 12s of the 95s e2e.

FIX: `uv tool install copier==9.16.0` once in the e2e session (or a session fixture that does it), then
invoke the installed binary rather than `uvx copier@...` per call.

KEEP THE PIN VISIBLE. The version currently travels in the `COPIER` constant read from conftest, and the
whole point of `uvx tool@version` here is that the pin is unmissable at the call site. Whatever replaces it
must still fail loudly if the installed version drifts from the pinned one — a silently-different copier
would make the e2e test something other than what consumers run.

Independent of f9y.2: this reduces the COST of a generation, that one reduces the NUMBER. They multiply.
close_reason: Installed once into a gitignored .e2e-tools/, version-checked against the pin each session and force-reinstalled on drift. Full e2e 101.97s -> 97.11s (~5s, against the bead's ~12s estimate — the call count was right, the 0.72s per-call figure was a cold measurement and warm resolution is cheaper). Also collapsed a second copy of the pin that lived in test_remote_update.py.

title: [bug] has_node() passes on Windows but subprocess cannot exec npx, so 3 jscpd e2e tests FAIL instead of skipping
description:
DONE. has_node() now invokes npx exactly as the tests do (subprocess with a fixed argv) and skips on
OSError/CalledProcessError, instead of asking shutil.which whether a name exists.

Verified: the probe returns False on this Windows box (which() found the extensionless POSIX script; exec
needs npx.cmd), and the three jscpd tests went 3 FAILED -> 3 SKIPPED, suite exit 0.

Generalised the lesson into the docstring: a capability guard has to exercise the same code path as the
thing it guards.

Note the noqa here is S607 alone, not S603 -- ruff reports S603 for a variable argv and S607 for a literal
partial path, and RUF100 caught the over-broad first attempt. A PATH lookup of a fixed argv IS the probe;
resolving the path would defeat it.

title: epic gate-run performance: startup dominates, not compute (measured)
description:
MEASURED TWICE, and the second measurement changed the picture. The first (2026-07-19) profiled OUR e2e
suite. The second profiled a REAL consumer repo, which is what users actually feel.

E2E SUITE (95s): dominated by copier, not by gates.
  one `uvx copier copy`        2.80s warm  (0.72s of that is uvx resolve + interpreter start)
  ~17 generations              ~48s of the 95s
  182 unit tests, full AST     ~5s total
The analyzers do not appear in the profile at all.

CONSUMER REPO — cardiac-seg, 102 files, the largest we have:
  import devtools           0.19s
  ClassIndex.multi_primary  0.07s      ClassArrows.edges   0.18s
  Demeter.violations        0.15s      CallArrows.edges    0.26s
  FeatureEnvy.violations    0.32s      Lcom.scan           0.11s
  CompositionCycles.cycles  0.18s      ImportGraph.build   0.03s
  ALL EIGHT ANALYZERS       ~1.3s of compute

  bare `uv run python -c pass`  0.318s
  So per gate: ~0.32s process + ~0.19s import + ~0.15s work.

THE CONCLUSION: analysis is roughly a QUARTER of a gate's cost. Startup is the rest — ~12 gates in a lint
run means ~6s of process+import overhead against ~1.3s of real work. Every lever below follows from that,
and it is why the Rust child sits at the bottom rather than the top.

LEVERS, by measured saving:

  f9y.1  pre-install copier          ~12s   trivial, independent of everything
  f9y.2  fewer e2e generations       ~34s   medium
  f9y.3  one-process gate run        ~5s per consumer lint, and it is the CONSUMER-facing one
  f9y.4  Rust/PyO3 analyzers         ~1s    very high effort

f9y.1 and f9y.2 are MULTIPLICATIVE, not alternatives: total = N x cost_per_generation. .1 reduces the cost,
.2 reduces N. Together 17 x 2.8s = 48s becomes ~5 x 2.1s = ~10s.
notes:
(Close reason continued — the original lost its second half to shell backtick substitution.)

Children are now standalone, carrying `performance` as a LABEL, which is what it always was:
  f9y.1 + f9y.2  our e2e suite's speed (label: dev-loop) — ~46s off a 95s run
  f9y.3          the SHIPPED gate runner, one process instead of twelve (consumer-visible)
  f9y.4          Rust/PyO3 — a platform change, deferred, filed at P4 by measurement

5cg (shared Resolver) was the one child genuinely delivered, in PR #25: contracts 85.6 -> 55.4 ms,
archmap 120.1 -> 64.9 ms.

Rule going forward: an epic names the THING CHANGED, not the property improved. If it cannot be finished,
it is a label.
close_reason:
DISSOLVED, not delivered. 'Performance' is a quality attribute, not a body of work — it cannot be finished, only pursued, so packaging it as an epic made its contents undoable as one sitting: pre-installing copier and a Rust/PyO3 rewrite share an adjective and nothing else.

Children are now standalone, carrying  as a LABEL, which is what it always was:
  f9y.1 + f9y.2  our e2e suite's speed (label: dev-loop) — ~46s off a 95s run
  f9y.3          the SHIPPED gate runner, one process instead of twelve (consumer-visible)
  f9y.4          Rust/PyO3 — a platform change, deferred, filed at P4 by measurement

5cg (shared Resolver) was the one child genuinely delivered, in PR #25: contracts 85.6 -> 55.4 ms, archmap 120.1 -> 64.9 ms.

Epics from here name the THING CHANGED, not the property improved.

title: contracts/composition re-walk the source tree ~4x per run (each engine builds its own Resolver)
description:
contracts.py constructs BOTH ClassArrows and CallArrows, and each constructs its own Resolver, which walks + parses the whole package tree to build the class-home index. composition.py then builds ClassArrows again, and archmap builds all three. So a full gate run parses the tree several times over.

Correct, just wasteful - and it scales with repo size, on gates that run on every commit via pre-commit. Fix: share one built graph / Resolver across the engines in a single run (an explicit shared context, or memoise the class-home index per package set). Worth measuring first on a real repo (cardiac) rather than optimising blind - the walk may be cheap next to the subprocess startup that dominates each gate.
close_reason:
One Resolver built per run and shared, rather than each engine building its own (and re-walking the tree on top). Resolver now materialises self.trees once; ClassArrows/CallArrows take an optional resolver; ClassIndex takes the TREES (it only ever needed the walk — injecting a Resolver would make a standalone run build a name index it never reads). contracts/graph/archmap each build one and pass it down.

MEASURED, not assumed — devtools tree, best of 3, against HEAD 4aa9dab in a worktree:
  contracts.edges()    85.6 ms -> 55.4 ms  (-35%)
  archmap.graph_data() 120.1 ms -> 64.9 ms (-46%)
archmap's number understates it: it now ALSO builds the MRO index and emits method edges.

Caveat consistent with the f9y epic: this is compute, and startup dominates (~0.32s process + 0.19s import per gate). So this is real but small next to f9y.3's one-process run — which is the point of the epic's ordering.

title: arrows.py drops self-edges, so self-composition is invisible
description:
ClassArrows.edges() filters 'target != src', so a class holding an instance of ITSELF never produces a holds edge - and therefore never forms an SCC>1 for the composition-cycle gate (4bl.4) to catch.

Often legitimate (tree/linked-list nodes, a Composite), which is why it was not just 'fixed'. But it is currently an UNDOCUMENTED gap rather than a decision: a self-owning class is invisible to the object graph either way.

Decide and record: either emit self-edges and let the cycle gate treat a 1-node SCC with a self-loop as reportable (with an opt-out for genuine recursive structures), or document the exclusion explicitly in the SPEC row so it is a stated boundary rather than an accident.
close_reason: DECIDED AND RECORDED (the bead asked for either). arrows.py now EMITS self-arrows — resolve_all lost its exclude param, since whether a self-arrow is wanted is the consumer's question, not resolution's. composition.py's cycles() docstring states the boundary explicitly: len(component) > 1 IS the rule, because a recursive type (tree node, linked list, Composite) can be built and tested alone while a mutually-owning pair cannot. So a self-owning class is now visible in the object graph and does not block. devtools itself has 0 self-arrows; the viewer draws a genuine one but drops a pair that merely folded onto the same ancestor.

title: drop the test_ prefix from mirror test filenames (convention migration)
description:
FROM REVIEW on PR #21: 'no need for the test_ prefix for mirror structure i think' -> 'maybe its not important enough right now. file a beads task for it. simple but lots of changes'. Exactly right: each edit is trivial, the blast radius is not.

The prefix is load-bearing in TWO places:
1. OUR OWN MIRROR GATE requires it - graph.py:137 builds the expected path as tests/unit/<pkg>/<path>/test_<name>.py. Drop the prefix and every module reads as unmirrored, so the arch gate goes red everywhere at once.
2. PYTEST DISCOVERY defaults to test_*.py. Files without it are simply not collected unless python_files is overridden - and a silently-uncollected test suite is worse than a red one.

So it is a 3-part migration, not a rename: (a) the mirror convention itself (a [tool.structure] test_layout value + graph.py's _missing_test), (b) pytest config (python_files) in the template pyproject, (c) every existing test file - in the scaffold AND the three consumer repos.

CONSUMER COORDINATION is the real cost: cardiac / mindscape / synthscape would each fail the mirror gate on their next copier update unless the rename lands in the same bump. Sequence it as one release, or ship the gate accepting BOTH layouts for one version and tighten after.

Worth doing for flatter names (tests/unit/<pkg>/store.py mirrors <pkg>/store.py exactly), just not urgent.
notes:
PULLED UNDER bd mjo 2026-07-21 (consolidation audit). This and bd ccy both rewrite every test file in the
scaffold, and this one also changes the path convention the new mirror gate (bd c6b) resolves against.

They are NOT merged -- this migration has a consumer-coordination cost that the mjo work does not (three
repos would fail the mirror gate on their next copier update), and mjo deliberately does not touch
consumer repos. Keeping them separate keeps that blast radius contained.

What the audit changes: c6b must read the mirror layout from `[tool.structure] test_layout` rather than
hardcoding the `test_` prefix. With that, this bead and the mjo conversion are independent and can land in
either order. Without it, whichever lands second rewrites the other's work.

If both are done in one pass, do the RENAME first and the restructure second -- renaming a file is
mechanical, restructuring its contents is not, and doing the mechanical half first keeps the diff readable.
close_reason:
Done in the mjo pass. The rename landed as a fourth test_layout VALUE rather than a new setting --
devtools/layout.py owns the convention as one strategy, and mirror/bare differ only in the prefix, so the
path arithmetic has one home and the two mirror gates cannot drift.

sdlc-devtools converted to bare. The shipped DEFAULT stays "mirror": switching costs a consumer a red gate
on every module at once, which is not a cost the scaffold gets to spend for anyone. misconfigured() fails
the gate when a bare tree lacks python_files, because an uncollected suite reports green and would
otherwise pass this gate too.

The rename immediately corrected a test asserting `python -m devtools.test_cli` -- a module that has never
existed, which read as correct only because the prefix made the mirror's name differ from its subject's.

title: Golden-fixture test convention: shared-builder generator + committed regen entrypoint + oracle pairing
description: First CHECK whether the scaffold already ships any guidance on golden/characterization tests (grep found none obvious). If absent, add a documented convention (template docs + optional helper) so consuming repos do it uniformly. The rules, from the cardiac-seg PR #45 discussion: (1) golden fixtures are DATA (JSON/npz under tests/.../fixtures/), regenerated by a COMMITTED entrypoint (a  or ) that SHARES the test's input builders (no dup) and CALLS THE REAL code under test (never a reimplementation) — deleting the generator (as done in cardiac yxg2) violates the 'one-off run -> committed command' rule. (2) State the SEMANTICS explicitly in the convention: a golden is a CHARACTERIZATION/regression test — it proves behavior did not CHANGE (bit-fidelity across the whole composed pipeline, incl RNG draw ORDER), NOT that behavior is CORRECT. Frozen-bug risk is real. (3) So the convention pairs golden with INDEPENDENT correctness oracles (property asserts: z-score=>std~1, labels in-range, finite; physics/first-principles unit asserts on transforms) so the frozen state is a VALIDATED state, not just 'whatever the code emitted'. (4) Note the equivalence: a full-chain fixed-seed EXACT-VALUE e2e test IS a golden; the fixture form is just packaging for outputs too big to inline. Deliverable = a scaffold doc section (+ maybe a tiny pytest --update-golden helper) capturing this so all repos share one workflow. Origin: cardiac-seg PR #45 review, yxg2 golden.
close_reason: Owner declined goldens as a pattern (cardiac-seg PR#45 — removed the one golden). No golden-fixture convention wanted; prefer value-bearing unit/integration/e2e tests in the tier hierarchy. drn (shape-everywhere) is unaffected/kept.

title: Document the classmethod migration path for op-namespace repos hitting SLF001 (v1.12)
description: v1.12 graduated SLF001 into the enforced ruff union (8ex reopened). But the scaffold ALSO mandates the op-namespace pattern via the ast-grep py-top-level-function rule: every helper must live on its owning class as a staticmethod (no free top-level defs). That pattern STRUCTURALLY produces SLF001 — a staticmethod (or a module main()) calling a sibling private staticmethod Cls._helper() reads as private-member-access. In mindscape this fired 102 SLF001 across core+neuroscan on the v1.10->v1.12 update, all the mandated pattern, none real encapsulation breaks. The two rules contradict: py-top-level-function forces the exact access SLF001 forbids. Current workaround = each adopting repo carves SLF001 back out tree-wide in the per-file-ignores LOCAL-SLOT, which defeats the graduation for op-namespace repos. Options for the scaffold: (a) don't graduate SLF001 while py-top-level-function is enforced (revert to advisory, the v1.0 8ex resolution); (b) ship the tree-wide SLF001 carve as a template default for repos that enable the op-namespace rule; (c) narrow SLF001 to CROSS-class access only (same-class Cls._x is the mandated idiom, OtherCls._y is the real smell) — but ruff can't express that distinction. Recommend (a) or (b): the mandate and the rule can't both be enforced as-is. Found via mindscape copier update to v1.12 (carved locally, documented as the 8ex conflict).
design: RESOLUTION (owner note in copier.yml, 2026-07-16): the premise 'py-top-level-function MANDATES Cls._helper access, conflicting with SLF001' is REFUTED. The two rules are independent axes — py-top-level-function only requires 'a helper lives on a class', NOT static-vs-instance, NOT private naming, NOT class-name calls. SLF001 is a real reach-in signal and STAYS enforced. The wall of hits an op-namespace repo sees on v1.12 comes from ONE self-imposed style: stateless @staticmethod + sibling calls written Cls._helper (a staticmethod has no self, so same-class calls must name the class -> ruff, which does no class-scope analysis, flags them as reach-ins). NOT a rule bug. FIX = @classmethod + cls._helper: cls is allowlisted so same-class calls go clean, the class stays STATELESS (no instance/state), and EXTERNAL callers do NOT change (a classmethod is still called Cls.method(x)). Proven on mindscape (102 hits / 30 files converted this way; genuine cross-module Other._private reaches stay flagged and get promoted to public API — the rule doing its job). ACTION for the scaffold: add this classmethod migration recipe to the adopter docs / SPEC so the next op-namespace repo updating past v1.12 knows the compliant pattern instead of reaching for a tree-wide SLF001 carve.
notes: VALIDATED end-to-end on mindscape (PR #66): 102 SLF001 across ~30 files -> 0 with NO per-file carve, nox lint green (SLF001 enforced + shape_contracts --assert), pytest 332 passed. The migration recipe that worked, in order: (1) op-namespace @staticmethod -> @classmethod, same-class sibling calls -> cls._x (external callers unchanged); (2) module-level def main() -> @classmethod main(cls) inside its class, guard -> Cls.main(); (3) cross-module-shared privates PROMOTED to public API (the real reach-ins) — and a newly-public array boundary then needs a jaxtyping shape if shape_contracts is on --assert; (4) library-private monkeypatches keep a # noqa: SLF001. Parallelizes well across files (mechanical + ruff self-verify). Recommend shipping this as the adopter-doc migration guide so op-namespace repos updating past v1.12 don't reach for a carve.
close_reason: Shipped docs/UPGRADING.md with the v1.12 SLF001 op-namespace migration recipe (@classmethod + cls._helper), linked from README + SPEC. PR #10 merged. Docs-only, no version bump.

title: devtools naming: unify archmap / archviz / architecture triple
description: One feature, three names: module devtools/archmap.py, vendored assets devtools/archviz/, output dir docs/architecture/. Owner: UNIFY to one word (align module + assets dir + output + docs + CLI). Bigger devtools change (renames the package dir, the wheel-shipped assets, write_viewer/write_json paths, the pre-commit + pages wiring, template .gitignore) -> its OWN release + a consumer copier update. P3, deferred. RESOLVED separately (aqs cheap part, done): stale sdlc-devtools/_site/ gitignore removed; _site CI staging name KEPT (GH Pages convention).
close_reason: Won't-do. The three names are not synonyms — they name three distinct LAYERS of the feature: archmap = compute (module/CLI that maps the import graph), archviz = render (vendored cytoscape viewer assets), architecture = output (the docs/ artifact + Pages path). Unifying to one stem would lose the compute/render/output distinction for a cosmetic gain and cost a breaking rename across the wheel + every consumer's committed graph.json path. Self-descriptive names win. Cheap part (stale _site gitignore) already done.

title: Investigation: magic_literals enforced count-ratchet as a BASE gate (not repo-CI-local)
description: Gap from cardiac-seg adoption (xwji audit). Parent: vip epic. 7g0 shipped magic_literals ADVISORY (report-only); the template noxfile says a repo opts into the --max-strings/--max-key-sets ratchet in its OWN CI = repo-local. cardiac runs it ENFORCED (--max-strings 48 --max-key-sets 11) as a merge gate. title: Instability / main-sequence coupling gate in graph.py (I = Ce/(Ce+Ca), distance D)
description:
Near-future. Sharpen the R3 coupling axis with Robert Martin`s package metrics — graph.py already computes the raw afferent/efferent counts, so this is additive.

WHAT graph.py HAS: Ca (afferent = fan-in), Ce (efferent = fan-out), betweenness, on the grimp import graph. Enforces direction (import-linter), acyclicity (no cycles), degree (god-module = Ca AND Ce both high).

WHAT TO ADD:
- Instability I = Ce / (Ce + Ca) per module/package (0 = maximally stable/depended-on, 1 = maximally unstable/depends-on-all). Trivial from the counts already gathered.
- (optional, needs an abstractness signal) Abstractness A = abstract-types / total-types; main-sequence distance D = |A + I - 1| — how far a package sits from the ideal stable<->abstract line (the "V"/stable-dependencies principle: arrows point toward stable abstractions).
- Report: a ranked I (and D if A is computed) table in the graph.py EXPLORER output (drop --assert), beside the existing fan-in/out/bottleneck/chokepoint tables.
- Gate: ADVISORY first (a number to watch), ratchet-to-blocking candidate — e.g. "a module moved further from the main sequence than its recorded baseline" (a per-module D ceiling in [tool.structure], same ratchet shape as file_max / magic-literals). Decide the exact assertion with owner before blocking.

WHY IT`S ITS OWN GATE, not god-module: god-module is a DEGREE check (both counts high). Instability is a RATIO/direction check — it catches a module that is unstable (high Ce) yet widely depended-on (high Ca would lower I, so specifically: depended-on things that are themselves volatile), the stable-dependencies violation a degree threshold misses. Complements, doesn`t duplicate.

OPEN: (a) abstractness signal for A/D — Python has no formal abstract/concrete split; approximate via ABC/Protocol/typing-only modules? or ship I only and defer A/D. (b) module vs package granularity. (c) advisory-forever vs D-ratchet; baseline capture. (d) does this belong in the scaffold superset for all repos or ml-only (it`s domain-neutral -> all repos).

Grounding: README "Coupling, in the standard vocabulary" section; Martin, Agile Software Development (SDP/SAP, the main sequence). Origin: owner insight while rewriting the README pyramid (R3 coupling axis). Relates to the convergence epic vip (a new house-bar gate -> rolls out through the pipe once landed).
close_reason: Done v0.16.0: Martin stability metrics in graph.py. instability(g) I=Ce/(Ce+Ca) (in_degree=Ca, out_degree=Ce; edges are importer->imported); abstractness(mod) A=abstract/total classes via AST (_is_abstract: ABC/Protocol base, metaclass=ABCMeta, or @abstractmethod); main_sequence_distance(g) D=|A+I-1|. Both in the explorer report tables. ADVISORY gate _off_main_sequence via [tool.structure] main_sequence_max, default 0.0 = OFF (a concrete stable leaf legitimately sits at D~1 -> no honest universal threshold, opt-in per repo; same honesty as file_min). Advisory-first = safe to add mid-rollout (never blocks; repos get it non-blocking). Unit: instability ratio, _is_abstract 3 forms, abstractness from file + None cases, distance + off-by-default. e2e: threshold ships. README + devtools README + SPEC updated.

title: Tier 3: reconcile the divergent ruff ratchet across the 3 repos (ambiguous placement)
description:
Parent: sdlc-scaffold-vip. Tier 3 = the gap that is REAL but has NO 3-repo consensus yet on whether/how it becomes house-bar. Filed so its tracked, NOT dropped and NOT declared permanent-local (per owner: nothing is permanently project-local; everything should flow through the pipe eventually — this is the "not yet decided where" bucket).

THE DIVERGENCE (audited 2026-07-14): the 3 repos ruff select ratcheted in DIFFERENT directions off the scaffolds narrow common floor:
- synthscape: scaffolds EXACT narrow list (no ratchet).
- mindscape: broad FAMILIES — C4, UP, T20, PLR, PLC, RUF, SIM, C90 (advisory target; enforced subset grows). Deliberately REJECTED N (383 hits).
- cardioseg: ~20 SPECIFIC codes — N (+ a [tool.ruff.lint.pep8-naming] ignore-names block), SLF001, PERF401, C408/C420, S603/S607, PTH123, E741/E742/E743, PLR0124, PLR1714, PLW3301, ICN001, E402, RUF005/007/010/012/022/046, PLW0108, E731.

WHY AMBIGUOUS: these are mostly 1/3 (cardioseg-only) or conflicting (mindscape broad-family vs cardioseg specific-code; N is ON in cardioseg, REJECTED in mindscape). By the scaffolds superset rule (majority of 3) NONE clears the bar today except SIM (already promoted in Tier 1). Promoting cardioseg`s taste wholesale = fitting the house bar to one repo — the exact anti-pattern the rule forbids.

THE WORK (a decision pass, not code-first):
1. Per-code triage across the 3: for each divergent code, is it (a) a bug-finder all 3 should run (-> promote to superset, roll out), (b) opinionated style that conflicts (-> pick ONE house answer WITH owner, or leave out), or (c) genuinely project-specific (-> a LOCAL-SLOT mechanism in the template, identical mechanism / different contents — e.g. the pep8-naming ignore-names is per-repo VOCAB but the block itself can be a shared slot).
2. Resolve the broad-family (mindscape) vs specific-code (cardioseg) STYLE clash — the scaffold must pick one expression of the select so generated config is identical. This is the core "generic architecture identical" tension.
3. Candidates likely to graduate as adoption widens (revisit each as a 2nd/3rd repo picks it up): SLF001, PERF401, the RUF safe-fix set, C408/C420. N + pep8-naming: decide the house line (cardioseg yes / mindscape no) — probably a domain=ml ML-idiom slot rather than a blanket N.
4. Output: an updated scaffold ruff_select (single source) + a pep8-naming LOCAL-SLOT mechanism, then the copier-update rollout so all 3 reconverge onto the SAME select (their only difference = LOCAL-SLOT vocab).

This is intentionally the slow tier — start Tier 1+2, widen adoption, come back and let evidence (2nd/3rd-repo uptake) decide each code. Origin: cardiac-seg ruff bd l79o / l5q8; mindscape + synthscape pyproject audits.
notes:
POST-ADOPTION UPDATE (mindscape adopted v0.10 on 2026-07-14, bd mindscape-588n): mindscapes line in the divergence above is now STALE — on adoption it took the scaffolds narrow curated select and thereby DROPPED its broad-family advisory + 3 enforced codes. Rule-loss cross-check vs pre-adoption main quantified the drop:
- ENFORCED lost: S101 (no assert in prod), W (pycodestyle warn), E7 (statement-level), T203 (pprint; T20->T201 keeps print/T201). LIVE IMPACT = ZERO — ruff over core+neuroscan+baselines with select S101,W,E7 is clean today. Theoretical only (a future prod assert would go un-gated).
- ADVISORY lost: the broad families (C4/UP/full E/W/PLR/PLC/S/RUF/C90) that fed the y63 graduate-one-family-per-PR ratchet. 6 live advisory items now HIDDEN from the statistics backlog: UP037 x3 (quoted-annotation), C416, C420, UP035 (deprecated-import). mindscapes whole y63 broad-advisory-then-graduate narrative is now moot under the fixed curated select.
So mindscape is now a 2nd repo on the scaffolds narrow list (with synthscape) — the broad-family expression lost a vote. The Tier-3 decision (this task) should consciously rule on: (a) does S101 belong in the house bar (asserts-in-prod is a real axis, 0-cost to re-add since all 3 are clean)? (b) is the fixed-curated-select model replacing the graduate-ratchet the intended house direction (kills y63s mechanism)? (c) the UP/C4 modernization hints — surface via an advisory-only broad slot, or drop by design (copier.yml already calls UP/C4 opinionated churn)? Owner rule: nothing silently lost — on record here for a conscious call, not accepted by default.
RESCOPED by o70: NOT 'parked pending consensus' anymore. Under UNION, cardiacs N / SLF001 / PERF401 / C408-420 / S603-607 / PTH123 / E741-3 / PLR0124-1714 / ICN001 / E402 ALL enter the union base select; every repo FIXES up (mindscapes N-rejection OVERRULED by union, not negotiated — k8c no-shortcuts). Broad-family (mindscape C4/UP) vs specific-code (cardiac): union the RULES regardless; codes-not-families is a presentation choice. SCAFFOLD-SIDE LANDED (v0.14.0): ruff_select is now the UNION = cardiac's full ratchet, byte-identical base — N, E741-3, PLR0124/1714, PLW3301, RUF005/007/010/012/022/046, C408/420, PERF401, PLW0108, E731, E402, ICN001, S603/607, PTH123, SLF001 (on top of narrow floor + SIM). Added the pep8-naming ignore-names FACT slot + the widened tests per-file-ignores carve-out (SLF001/N8xx/PLR0913). JUDGMENT CALLS ON RECORD: OUT = UP (annotation churn), E701/702 (dense compaction is house style), W (formatter owns it); S101 (no-assert-in-prod) LEFT OUT pending owner's conscious call — a real axis all 3 honor by convention, not added by default. REMAINING (keep open): the per-repo FIX rollout is consumer beads (mindscape's 383 N-hits fixed no-shortcuts; synthscape bump) + the S101 owner decision. Reconvergence 'done' = all 3 generate the identical select via copier update.

Rationale — assert is stripped under python -O so a prod invariant on a bare assert silently vanishes; all 3 repos honor it by convention (zero fix when clean). SCAFFOLD-SIDE OF vip.2 IS NOW COMPLETE: union select final (cardiac ratchet + S101), pep8-naming/vulture/per-file FACT slots all shipped. Proof: ruff select rule SET == the 41-code union in cardiac-seg, mindscape, synthscape (byte-verified; cardiac has S101/S110 transposed in order only — set-identical, heals on next render). mindscape's 383 N-hits FIXED (N is in the union, tree clean). synthscape ruff enforced (union + F722 ignore) = All checks passed.
