title: two is_property implementations with different semantics
description:
`purity.PropertyPurity.is_property` EXCLUDES setters and deleters -- it asks "is this a pure read", and the
setter is that gate's declared exception.

`mirror.MethodMirror.is_property` INCLUDES them -- it asks "how is this member exercised", and all three
answer "by attribute access".

Both are correct for their own question, and mirror.py's docstring says so explicitly. Recording it anyway
because a reader meeting the second one will reasonably ask why it is not reused, and because a future
change to either could quietly assume they agree.

TWO HONEST OUTCOMES: leave them and let the docstring carry it (probably right -- they are different
predicates that happen to share a name), or rename one so the names stop implying they answer the same
question (`is_property_read` vs `is_property_member`, or similar).

P4: nothing is wrong today. It is a naming collision between two true statements.

title: PARKED: integration/e2e test doctrine (needs expertise not yet here)
description:
DELIBERATELY PARKED, not rejected. Filed so the material is findable rather than lost in a transcript.

Owner's reasoning (2026-07-21): "i am most experienced with unit tests and feel i know what should be
there. but people who worked on the other levels know them too." The scaffold gates what its author has
the expertise to gate well. Same rule already applied to consumers -- the scaffold ships, downstream
agents own their own integration.

WHAT IS PARKED, all recorded in research/deep_dives/2026-07-21_test_architecture_sota.md:

  PER-JOINT CONTRACT TESTING (section 4b). "A's output is valid input to B" -- shape, dtype, range,
  schema. Already a stated house rule in CLAUDE.md's testing section, never gated. Attractive because it
  asserts no VALUES, so it cannot reimplement production and cannot collapse into a tautology.

  END-TO-END INVARIANTS over a canonical example (section 4b). Labels preserved, no NaN introduced,
  deterministic under a fixed seed, output shape derivable from input shape. The canonical chain doubles
  as proof the pipeline COMPOSES, which per-unit tests structurally cannot show.

  THE CROSS-LEVEL HALF OF THE STANDARD FIXTURE IDEA (section 4b). One canonical data story flowing
  through unit, integration and e2e, with WHAT IS ASSERTED changing by level. The unit half is in bd ccy;
  this is the rest.

  GOOGLE'S SIZE AXIS ABOVE THE UNIT TIER (section 3b). medium = may call localhost; large = may span
  machines. bd ar6 gates only the SMALL end. Nothing expresses the boundary between medium and large.

UNPARK WHEN: someone with real integration/e2e experience is available to judge these, or a consumer repo
sends a ticket up asking for one. Do not design these from first principles here -- that is how the unit
work stayed sharp and this would not.

title: gate type PLACEMENT: a type declared in A but produced only in B
description:
From acq observation 1. CLAUDE.md states the rule — a supplementary type lives BESIDE its producer, never
bucketed by KIND (`*_types.py`, `schema.py`) — and no axis expresses it, so mirage accumulated two bucket
modules that passed every gate: well-formed, not dead, not cyclic, and NOT god-modules (fan-in high but
fan-out ~0, so that rule's AND never fires). Visible only to a human reading the graph.

The signal is precise and buildable from what already exists (arrows.py / calls.py / classes.py):
  "type T is declared in module A but constructed/returned only in module B" -> single producer, misplaced.
  Multiple producers = genuinely shared vocabulary, belongs at the package root, SKIP.

A weaker bucket smell as a second rule: N types, zero behaviour, imported by K modules of its own package,
not __init__, not a dispatch table.

CARVE-OUTS that must exist or it over-fires (both stated in acq, both real): shared vocabulary consumed by
several producers, and declaration-only CLI dispatch tables.

Evidence it is worth doing: dissolving both buckets in mirage was a NET LINE REDUCTION and surfaced design
facts they had hidden — a duplicate narrow view of a payload (twice), and a placement that resolved by
import direction because the reverse was a hard cycle.

Advisory first, measured on all four consumer repos before it blocks.
notes:
NAMING CORRECTION: the evidence repo  is  on GitHub — one repo, not a fourth consumer. Calibrate against the THREE consumer source trees (cardiac-seg, mindscape, synthscape), read-only: that is measurement, not enforcement. Updating those repos or triaging their findings is their agents' call, not the scaffold's.
(The previous note lost three words to shell backtick substitution — restated here.)

NAMING CORRECTION: the evidence repo "mirage" is "synthscape" on GitHub — one repo, not a fourth consumer.
There are THREE: cardiac-seg, mindscape, synthscape.

SCOPE: calibrate against those three source trees READ-ONLY. That is measurement, and it is in scope.
Updating those repos, or deciding what to do about a finding inside them, is their agents' call — the
scaffold ships the gate, it does not enforce adoption.
DESIGN HOLE, from review: the rule as written fires on FACTORIES and is wrong there.

  ThingFactory (factory.py) constructs Thing (thing.py). Single producer, different module -> the rule
  says "move Thing into factory.py". But a factory exists precisely to be the only constructor of
  something that stands on its own.

The existing carve-out (multiple PRODUCERS = shared vocabulary) does not help: a factory IS a single
producer. The missing discriminator is REFERENCE BREADTH rather than construction site.

  XResult   built by X, and NAMED only in X's own signatures            -> narrow, belongs beside X
  Thing     built by ThingFactory, NAMED in many modules' annotations   -> wide, has its own home

So: if any module OTHER than the constructor references the type in a signature, it is not misplaced.
The intended target was always the supplementary type — a result, a config, the private shape of one
function's return — not any type with a single construction site.

Computable today: `references` (class -> class via signature annotations) is already emitted by arrows.py,
so the check is "constructed in exactly one module AND referenced by no other module".

CARVE-OUT LIST is now three: shared vocabulary (several producers), declaration-only dispatch tables, and
widely-referenced types with a single constructor (factories). Measure each against the three consumer
source trees read-only before the rule goes past advisory.
KILLED BY MEASUREMENT (acq audit, 2026-07-20). Two independent reasons:

1. No base rate. The kind-bucket detector (>=3 classes, zero def) returns ZERO across cardiac-seg,
   mindscape, synthscape and devtools. The only two instances ever seen were dissolved during the v1.15.0
   adoption that reported them; nothing has re-accumulated in the months since.

2. No precise rule. The proposed signal — "type declared in A, constructed/returned only in B" — fires 45
   times across the four trees, and the hits are the scaffold OWN recommended patterns: registry+adapter
   (CmrxMotionAdapter/Mnm2Adapter/Mnms1Adapter/ScdAdapter all declared per-module and built in
   registry.py), strategy objects built by the trainer, and models built only by their experiment entry
   point. devtools trips it too (Switch: cli -> graph). Precision would have to be bought back with
   carve-outs for registries, ABC subclasses and entry-point scripts, at which point the rule is a pile of
   exceptions rather than a principle.

Left OPEN at P4 rather than closed: the SMELL is real (a human reading the graph sees it), what is missing
is a mechanism that separates it from a registry. If someone finds that discriminator, the work is here.
CORRECTION + CLOSE (2026-07-20). The note above conflated two rules that died for different reasons, and
one of the reasons was wrong.

  1b (type declared in A, constructed only in B) — dead on MEASUREMENT. 45 hits across four trees, the
     dominant shapes being registry+adapter, strategy, and model-built-by-entry-point. The predicate cannot
     separate "stranded away from its producer" from "deliberately built by a dispatcher", so its findings
     are mostly the patterns the scaffold teaches. Wrong rule, correctly killed.

  1 (kind-bucket: >=3 classes, zero `def`, not __init__) — NOT killed by measurement. It returns zero on
     all four trees, and the earlier "no base rate, nothing to ratchet against" reasoning was bad: a valid
     rule at zero is a rule that ships BLOCKING on day one and holds the line, which is the shape_contracts
     precedent, not a weakness. The two instances it was built from (surfscan/deploy/schema.py at 200 lines
     / 19 symbols / zero def / five importers, and evaluation/result_types.py) were real, invisible to
     every other axis, and dissolving them was a net line reduction.

     Closed anyway, by owner decision: not worth a gate. Recording the reasoning so this is not re-derived
     from scratch — the smell is real and the detector is ~15 lines, but nobody is asking for it.

Closing the whole bead. Both halves stay dead.
close_reason: owner decision: both halves stay dead. 1b is a wrong predicate (45 FPs, flags registry/strategy/entry-point); the kind-bucket half is a workable blocking gate but not wanted. Reasoning preserved in notes.

title: Rust/PyO3 rewrite of the analyzers
description:
LOW PRIORITY BY MEASUREMENT, not by taste. Filed so the idea is recorded with the numbers that argue
against it, rather than being re-proposed from intuition.

WHAT IT WOULD BUY: ~1s. All eight analyzers on cardiac-seg (102 files) total ~1.3s of compute, and 182 unit
tests with full AST walks run in ~5s. Compute is not where the time goes.

WHAT ACTUALLY COSTS: startup. ~0.32s process + ~0.19s import per gate, ~12 gates per lint run, so ~6s of
overhead against ~1.3s of work. A Rust rewrite attacks the 1.3s and leaves the 6s untouched.

AND THE STARTUP WIN DOES NOT REQUIRE RUST: f9y.3 gets most of it in Python by running the gates in one
process. Cheaper to build, no per-platform wheels, no build step.

SO THE ONLY HONEST ARGUMENT FOR RUST is a single fast-starting DISTRIBUTED BINARY — the ruff model — where
the win is startup and distribution, not compute. That is a packaging decision, and it trades against the
scaffold's selling point: every tool is `uvx tool@version`, no build, no platform matrix. Revisit only if
(a) f9y.3 has landed and startup still dominates, or (b) a consumer repo appears where analysis time itself
is measurable — neither is true today.

If it is ever done, port the hot engine only (measure which) behind the same report()/run_assert()
interface, so the Python implementation stays the reference and the two can be differentially tested.
